title: Strawberry Fool
subtitle: Crushed macerated strawberries folded through whipped double cream
excerpt: Fresh strawberries macerated with sugar, half of them crushed to a rough sauce, the other half left in pieces, folded through softly whipped double cream. Served cold in glasses. One of the most straightforward British summer desserts.
sourceNotes: The fool is one of the oldest British dessert forms, with references to cream and fruit preparations appearing in English cookery from the sixteenth century onward. The name is thought to derive from the French 'fouler' (to crush or press), which describes the basic technique. Strawberry fool became the most popular version as British strawberry culture developed in the nineteenth century.

content.text: A good strawberry fool needs good strawberries. This is not a recipe that hides poor fruit. The strawberry flavour has to carry the whole dessert. The sugar step is not optional. Leaving the fruit with sugar for 30 minutes draws out the juice and lifts the flavour in a way that adding sugar to cream never quite does.

Leave the cream at soft peaks, thick enough to hold the fruit in suspension but not stiff. A stiff cream makes the fool heavy and dry. The finished texture should be loose and barely holding when spooned into a glass.

Halve or quarter {{strawberries}} of hulled strawberries and toss with {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar and the juice of {{lemon}}. Leave for 30 minutes at room temperature, stirring once. The sugar will draw out the juice.

Set aside about a third of the macerated strawberries with their juice. Crush the rest roughly with a fork to a coarse, uneven sauce with visible pieces. Whip {{double-cream}} of double cream to soft peaks in a large bowl. Add the crushed strawberry mixture and fold gently until combined. Fold through the reserved whole pieces, keeping them visible.
Spoon into glasses and chill for at least 1 hour. Serve with shortbread or plain biscuits.

items.fix: Strain some of the juice off the macerated strawberries before crushing and folding. Keep the juice separately and drizzle it over at the table.
items.cause: Strawberries were very juicy and released too much liquid into the cream
items.symptom: Fool is too thin and watery

items.fix: Whip cream to soft peaks only. Fold the fruit in quickly and get the glasses chilled immediately. Do not leave the mixture sitting in a warm kitchen.
items.cause: Over-whipped before folding, or the acid in the fruit curdled the cream
items.symptom: Cream has separated and gone grainy

items.fix: Leave the fruit with sugar for the full 30 minutes, this step cannot be rushed. If the strawberries were not ripe, add a little extra lemon juice and sugar to bring out what flavour is there.
items.cause: Strawberries were not ripe, or the sugar step was skipped

items.fix: Taste the sugared fruit before folding. If sweet enough without the full 50 g of sugar, use less. The lemon juice helps balance the sweetness.
items.cause: Strawberries were very ripe and the sugar amount was too high

content.text: The fool is one of the oldest British dessert forms, with references appearing in English cookery from the sixteenth century. The word is thought to derive from the French 'fouler', to crush, which describes the basic technique: fruit crushed and folded through cream. Gooseberry was the original and most traditional fruit for the dish, but the availability of British strawberries from the nineteenth century onward made strawberry fool one of the most widely made versions. The dessert remains a British summer staple and requires no cooking at all.
